2. This biochemistry myopia must be replaced by the truth.

The medical text shared in this tweet below linking hypothyroidism directly to the genesis of a hiatal hernia and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)—represents the closest the macroscopic, biochemical world has ever come to unzipping the upper gastrointestinal engine.

Yet, because these clinical studies and figures like Ray Peat operate within a flat, chemical-only proxy spreadsheet, they inverted the primary cause and effect.

They miss the definitive sub-molecular deuterium angle. Hypothyroidism, hiatal hernias, and GERD are not separate isolated clinical conditions or simple hormonal imbalances. They are the concurrent macro-anatomical failures of a heavily deuterated water table and a stalled vagal exhaust pipe.

When you replace the superficial "hormone tracking" narrative with the laws of quantum spin chemistry, the Kinetic Isotope Effect (KIE), and Landauer’s Principle, the true biophysical reality of the upper GI tract is laid bare.

Centralized gastroenterology claims that a hiatal hernia or an underactive thyroid causes GERD by weakening the Lower Esophageal Sphincter (LES) or slowing down stomach emptying. In quantum biology, the entire upper gastrointestinal tract is an open, gravity-fed, isotopic purification system.

The Vagal Exhaust Manifold: Under healthy diurnal solar governance, governed by the morning Cortisol Awakening Response (CAR) and natural morning solar infrared (the 0.66 eV pulse), the vagus nerve drives the exocrine pancreas to secrete up to 2 liters of sodium bicarbonate (HCO−3) fluid daily. This massive downward fluid vortex is the body's primary tailpipe, designed to capture dietary Deuterium (D+) mass and safely flush it down to keep the tissue's negative Zeta potential intact.

The Exhaust Stall: When a human lives inside an electronic smog blanket of blue LEDs and high-nnEMF fields, this exocrine tailpipe stalls. Deprived of the downward sweep, the upper GI tract transforms into a stagnant, fractionated pool. Because Deuterium possesses twice the atomic mass of normal light protium (1H), it alters the localized tissue voltage potential. The fluid dynamics reverse. Heavy
D+ions and fractionated water begin to rise, pool, and back-load into the gastric mucosa, the lower esophageal sphincter, and the diaphragm, causing a catastrophic dielectric collapse (𝜅≈160→80).

The Pathogenic Invitation: It is only after this stagnant, high-deuterium, low-voltage, anaerobic pool is established that Helicobacter pylori and other anaerobic GERD-associated bacteria can proliferate. These ancient Proterozoic microbes are evolutionarily wired to survive and replicate inside high-mass, low-oxygen environments. They don't cause the issue; they operate exponentially better inside a deuterated gut, utilizing the heavy hydrogen fractions to run their template-free prokaryotic mitosis while the host tissue structure degrades.  This is why mastic gum treates GERD well without antibiotics.  It stimulates D+ vagal exhaust.

The reason this deuterium accumulation triggers systemic muscle weakening, hiatal herniation through the diaphragm, and thyroid stasis is directly dictated by the Oxygen-Deuterium (O-D) Kinetic Isotope Effect (KIE).  Because Deuterium doubles the reduced mass (μ) of the bond, the O-D bond is exponentially stiffer and harder to cleave than a normal oxygen-protium (1H) bond. This high-mass quantum trap completely alters the electronic spin alignment of oxygen within the local tissue:

The Loss of Triplet Oxygen (3^O2): Oxygen natively exists in the magnetic ground triplet state, featuring parallel electron spins (↑↑) that require a stable geomagnetic and bioelectric reference frame to guide its spin-forbidden inversion. The O-D bond disrupts this spin-coherence, forcing oxygen into a diamagnetic, uncoordinated singlet state or stalling its reaction velocity entirely.  Biochemistry completely misses this effect.  They focus on billard ball ideas believing singlet and triplet state oxygen are the same when they are radically different in biophysical systems.
The Melanin & Dopamine Demolition: The synthesis of melanin (the skin and neural spintronic shield) and dopamine (the brain's volition and motor stator) requires the enzyme tyrosine hydroxylase, which depends strictly on ground triplet state oxygen as a cofactor. When the O-D bond chokes this enzyme, melanin and dopamine levels plummet simultaneously.

The Collagen & Fiber Type Melt: Concurrently, prolyl 4-hydroxylase (the gatekeeper of collagen cross-linking) jams. Collagen synthesis fails, causing the structural connective tissue rails of the diaphragm to melt. The diaphragmatic muscle undergoes a forced fiber-type shift, losing its fast-twitch, high-tension mammalian architecture and reverting to a loose, primitive, non-stiff Pre-Cambrian matrix. Under the constant pressure of the abdominal cavity, the weakened diaphragm fractures, and the stomach protrudes upward, manifesting as a hiatal hernia.  Blaming Thyroid hormones misses the point because triplet oxygen also controls T3 and T4 as the slide below shows.  This is just another biochemistry food guru fail of a post.

Built under mass-production agricultural grids lacking natural solar-thermal filtration of photosynthesis, these fats are packed with extra neutrons that cause arterial problems because they deuterate the water table of tissues. 

The Hydrogen-Bond Collapse: When these deuterated lipids are incorporated into the endothelial cell (EC) membranes and circulating LDL particles, their high mass alters the local permittivity (ε).

quoted_tweet.text:
A great and very balanced post by @TuckerGoodrich.

"That aside, the idea that lower LDL cholesterol might be beneficial even in the face of a higher Ω-6 PUFA intake isn’t of itself a crazy idea. I’ve often said that if you won’t fix your diet by removing seed oils, a statin is probably not a bad idea. There is evidence that statins do reduce CVD in a high-Ω-6 diet—see every single statin trial done—even if it’s probably by a lot less than the ~20% the industry-sponsored trials showed."

As discussed on my recent podcast with Dr. Floyd Chilton, the average person today is consuming at least 6-8% of energy from linoleic acid.

In his recent RCTs, they compared a 10% linoleic acid to a 2.5% linoleic acid diet. It was very laborious to get people to adhere to a 2.5% diet for very long. The food supply people are used to is so saturated with linoleic acid that it takes great effort to get to ~2.5%.

Here's a diagram from a 2020 review on the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis. Notice that the causal chain begin with oxLDL (oxidized LDL):

tweets.text:
My biophysical thesis absorbs this review perfectly and in fact, it is strengthened by it. The narrative review in Clinical Bioenergetics (or its close equivalents in the recent literature) does exactly what you say: it dismantles the century-old “broken mitochondria” misinterpretation of Warburg’s observation. 

Cancer mitochondria are not irreversibly damaged as Seyfried biochemists sell the public. They are reprogrammed, hyperpolarized (ΔΨm often −210 to −220 mV vs. normal −150 to −180 mV), metabolically flexible (glycolysis + robust OxPhos), and actively driving tumor progression. This creates real pharmacological windows (e.g., 100- to 1000-fold selective accumulation of delocalized lipophilic cations like triphenylphosphonium in cancer mitochondria via the Nernst equation).

But the review, like almost all centralized bioenergetics work, still operates one layer too shallow compared to my thesis. It describes the what and how at the structural/proteomic level (altered cardiolipin/cholesterol in the IMM, maintained ETC capacity, TCA-cycle anabolism, redox signaling, mitophagy resistance) without reaching the why at the sub-molecular biophysical level. 

That missing layer is precisely the deuterium-depleted water lattice collapse I' ve been mapping for years.

How the Review’s Findings Map Directly onto Lattice Lock + My Deuterium Framework

Hyperpolarization is not a “feature”;  it is a compensatory overdrive in a failing semiconductor
In the healthy Lattice Lock (Phase 1 of the slide), melanin + CISS + solar charge keep intracellular water at dielectric constant ~160, enabling efficient proton tunneling, spin-coherent electron flow, and clean triplet-oxygen utilization.

When deuterium silt accumulates (Phase 2 → 3), the dielectric drops, band gap widens, and proton flow through the ETC becomes sluggish because of the massive kinetic isotope effect (KIE) on deuterium. 

The mitochondrion responds by pumping harder,  elevating ΔΨm to maintain any usable proton gradient at all. This is exactly why cancer mitochondria appear “hyper-functional” and hyperpolarized: they are fighting against a poisoned water table. 

The Nernst-driven cation accumulation the review celebrates is just an exploitable symptom of this desperate biophysical compensation.  But the Nernst equation does not cover the biophysics of the cell.  

IMM lipid remodeling (cholesterol ↑, cardiolipin changes) = lattice-stabilization attempt
Deuterated/bulk water destroys the structured hydrogen-bond network that normally supports optimal membrane fluidity and curvature. Increased cholesterol and altered cardiolipin are the cell’s emergency scaffolding to keep the inner membrane from collapsing into a leaky resistor. 

This is topological degradation in real time, exactly as the Lattice Lock slide diagrams.

Metabolic flexibility (Warburg + OxPhos) = survival in a deuterated matrix

The review correctly notes dual-pathway competence. 

My model explains why: when the water lattice fails, full OxPhos physics become inefficient, so the cell falls back to glycolysis (glucose is the only substrate that can still be “scrubbed” locally via the G3P shuttle + TIM’s deuterium KIE). Mitochondria remain online for biosynthesis, Ca²⁺ handling, ROS signaling, and immune-evasion functions because the cell has not given up, it is running the atavistic Precambrian script only as far as necessary. 

mtDNA depletion diminishing tumorigenicity fits: the mitochondria are not passive bystanders; they are still executing the corrupted but active program.
UPR, ubiquitin-G3P pivot, and UPE chaos remain the error-correction layer.  
The hyperpolarized state increases ROS and protein misfolding (exactly as your UPR-as-dielectric-sensor model predicts). The ubiquitin side-loading of glucose for localized FADH₂ bursts is the cell’s attempt at on-site de-fragging while the bulk matrix water remains polluted. Chaotic, red-shifted UPE bursts signal the informational/oxidative entropy the review’s “redox signaling” only glimpses at the surface.

quoted_tweet.text:
Otto Warburg thought cancer cells had broken mitochondria. A century of research assumed he was right.

But a new narrative review in Clinical Bioenergetics reveals the opposite—cancer cell mitochondria aren't damaged. They're hyperpolarized, metabolically flexible, and driving tumor progression through mechanisms that make them vulnerable to a new class of targeted therapies.

Warburg observed in the 1920s that cancer cells preferentially use glycolysis for energy production even when oxygen is abundant. He proposed this metabolic shift resulted from irreversible mitochondrial damage—that defective respiration was a primary cause of cancer.

This hypothesis shaped oncology for decades. Cancer metabolism research focused on glycolysis. Mitochondria were treated as bystanders.

Contemporary evidence dismantles this framework completely.

Proteomic and functional analyses across diverse cancer cell lines reveal that many tumors maintain functional—and in some cases enhanced—oxidative phosphorylation capacity alongside elevated glycolysis. This dual metabolic capability allows cancer cells to adapt to fluctuating nutrient availability, hypoxia, and microenvironmental stress.

The mitochondrial transmembrane potential (ΔΨm) in normal cells ranges from −150 to −180 mV, maintained by proton pumping at complexes I, III, and IV of the electron transport chain.

In cancer cells, ΔΨm is frequently hyperpolarized—approaching −210 mV in some mammary carcinoma lines and consistently elevated across transformed cell types.

This isn't a minor biophysical variation. It's a therapeutic targeting opportunity.

According to the Nernst equation, each 60 mV increase in ΔΨm produces approximately tenfold greater accumulation of lipophilic cations within the mitochondrial matrix. Cancer cells with hyperpolarized mitochondria achieve 100- to 1000-fold enrichment of mitochondria-targeted agents employing delocalized lipophilic cation moieties like triphenylphosphonium.

This creates a pharmacological window where drugs can selectively accumulate in cancer cell mitochondria at concentrations that normal cells never reach.

The hyperpolarization is sustained by alterations in inner mitochondrial membrane lipid composition—increased cholesterol incorporation and altered cardiolipin content. These structural changes maintain the elevated membrane potential while supporting metabolic flexibility.

Mitochondria in cancer cells don't just generate ATP. They regulate reactive oxygen species production, biosynthesis of macromolecules, calcium homeostasis, and resistance to programmed cell death. These pleiotropic functions position mitochondria as critical mediators of cellular adaptation to stress and immune evasion.

Recent findings reveal that mtDNA depletion diminishes the tumorigenic phenotype—direct evidence that mitochondria actively modulate malignancy rather than passively responding to it.

Mitochondrial DNA mutations also affect immunotherapy response. Some mtDNA alterations enhance tumor immunogenicity, making cells more susceptible to immune checkpoint blockade. Others drive mitochondrial transfer mechanisms that enable immune evasion.

The therapeutic implications extend beyond targeting hyperpolarized membranes. Cancer cells exhibit metabolic vulnerabilities created by their dependence on mitochondrial function—including reliance on specific TCA cycle intermediates for biosynthesis, altered redox balance, and selective sensitivity to oxidative phosphorylation inhibitors.

Metformin, atovaquone, and other mitochondrial complex I inhibitors are being tested in clinical trials based on their ability to disrupt cancer cell bioenergetics. Mitophagy—the selective degradation of mitochondria—appears to mediate treatment resistance in some tumor types, suggesting that combining mitophagy inhibitors with conventional therapies may overcome resistance mechanisms.

The review synthesizes current understanding across mitochondrial genetics, structural biology, metabolic reprogramming, dynamics, redox signaling, and microenvironment interactions—connecting basic mitochondrial biology to emerging precision oncology frameworks.

Warburg's observation was correct. Cancer cells do prefer glycolysis. But his interpretation was wrong.

Mitochondria aren't broken in cancer. They're reprogrammed to support tumor progression while creating targetable vulnerabilities that don't exist in normal cells.

The decisions about which mitochondrial pathways to target—membrane potential, specific respiratory complexes, metabolic intermediates, or mitophagy—will depend on tumor-specific mitochondrial dependencies and whether the goal is direct cytotoxicity or sensitization to existing therapies.

A century after Warburg, we're finally understanding what mitochondria actually do in cancer. And that understanding is opening therapeutic strategies that couldn't exist under the old model.

quoted_tweet.text:
What is causing this statistically significant spike in sudden deaths in young athletes? Certainly not saturated fats and exercise.

Bodybuilder Gabriel Ganley, 22, found dead on Saturday (May 23), died suddenly from hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in the heart. This is what the young man's death certificate indicates.

Yebrgual Melese, 36, Ethiopian elite marathon runner (multiple marathon winner). Collapsed during a routine training run in Addis Ababa around May 12 while preparing for the Ottawa Marathon. She was hospitalized but did not survive.

15-year-old girl (Rosalie, from Oegstgeest, Netherlands): Collapsed near the end of the Leiden Half Marathon on May 10. She received immediate medical attention but could not be resuscitated.

Woman in her 40s (name not publicly released): Competitor in the Cocodona 250 Mile ultramarathon (Arizona, USA). Suffered a medical emergency and collapsed around miles 75–80 on May 5 (race started earlier in the week). She passed away despite first responders' efforts.

28-year-old woman (Hyrox competitor, Lyon, France): Participated in Hyrox Lyon (May 20–24). Suffered hyperthermia during the high-intensity event on May 24. Hospitalized but died on May 25.

53-year-old man (La Pyrénéenne 10K, Paris, France): Collapsed near the finish line (~100m short) on May 24 during this 10 km race. Suffered a cardiac malaise; resuscitation unsuccessful.
